display_name: Interface up / line protocol down means L1 link exists but L2 negotiation failed
context: show interface status showing interface state as up but line protocol as down
observation: Interface shows 'up' but line protocol shows 'down' — the two halves disagree
naive_interpretation: The cable is bad or the interface is malfunctioning
correct_interpretation: Interface up means the physical layer (L1) is seeing a signal — the link is electrically/optically present. Line protocol down means the data link layer (L2) negotiation failed. This is typically a speed/duplex mismatch, an encapsulation mismatch, a keepalive failure, or the remote end is administratively shut at L2 while still sending a carrier signal. The cable is usually fine — the problem is configuration or protocol negotiation.
why: IOS and NX-OS report L1 and L2 status independently — they can disagree
A completely dead cable or missing SFP would show both as down, not just line protocol
Common causes: speed/duplex mismatch on copper, LACP misconfiguration, encapsulation mismatch on WAN links, missing keepalives

confirmation_commands.purpose: Check speed/duplex negotiation result and any error messages
Check speed/duplex, encapsulation type, and keepalive configuration
If this is a port-channel member, check if LACP is failing to bundle

traps: Replacing cables when the L1 signal is actually present and healthy
Not checking the remote side for configuration mismatches
Assuming both directions of the link are in the same state — one side can be up/up while the other is up/down
